goal: MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME env-var fallback for mainline init

summary.title: MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME env-var fallback for mainline init
summary.what: When `mainline init` is run without --actor-name, read MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME from the environment before falling through to the internal default. Surface a one-line note in the success output when the env-var fallback fires so users know where the identity came from. Update the flag help text to reference the env var.
summary.why: Tomorrow's trial users may export MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME in their shell (the natural variable name for that value) and forget to thread --actor-name through the init invocation. Pre-this-fix the silent failure mode is: the user becomes 'default-agent' in every actor log and commit note, with the existing warning surfacing only after init has already created the wrong identity. Env-var fallback closes that gap without changing any existing behaviour.
summary.user_goal: A user with $MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME exported in their environment runs `mainline init` and gets the right actor identity automatically.

decisions.rationale: Explicit flag wins because that's the lowest-friction override (most specific signal). Env-var is the long-running shell convention. Internal default is the safety net so init still completes.

decisions.chose: Print '(actor name picked up from $MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME)' when the env-var fallback fires.
decisions.rationale: Silent fallbacks are how users get surprised by the wrong identity. One short line keeps the CLI behaviour predictable.

decisions.chose: Keep the existing 'No --actor-name passed' warning when BOTH flag and env-var are empty; mention the env-var as an alternative path in the warning text.
decisions.rationale: The warning was added to fix a known alpha-walkthrough surprise. The env-var fallback shrinks the surprise surface but doesn't eliminate it; users running with neither set still need the same nudge.

rejected.reason: Surprising defaults are worse than explicit ones. Users who want the shell username can set MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME=$USER once and be done. Falling through to $USER silently would mean a user expecting 'default-agent' (because they didn't set anything) would suddenly become 'huoru' on macOS.

rejected.alternative: Interactive prompt when --actor-name is missing AND stdin is a tty.
rejected.reason: Bigger UX change. Worth doing later, not for tomorrow.

rejected.alternative: Bake the env-var lookup into svc.Init itself rather than the CLI.
rejected.reason: Engine should not read env vars — that's a CLI concern. Mixing them couples library callers (future LLM runners, hooks) to the same env-var convention they may not want.

risks: If a user sets MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME for one repo and forgets to unset it before initialising another with a different identity, they'll get the env-var value silently. Mitigation: the success output explicitly prints which path the value came from.

anti_patterns.what: Adding env-var fallbacks to engine.Service methods
anti_patterns.why: Library callers (LLM runners, hooks, future SDKs) may legitimately not want env-var override. The CLI is the right layer for environment integration; the engine should take explicit arguments.

followups: Consider an interactive prompt when --actor-name is missing AND env-var is unset AND stdin is a tty. Lower priority than tomorrow's trial.
Audit other CLI commands for env-var convenience (sync, check) — but be conservative; env-var creep is its own footgun.

architectural_claims: Env-var integration belongs at the CLI boundary, not in engine.Service. Engine takes explicit arguments.
Fallback order is documented and shown to the user; silent fallbacks are how users get surprised.
behavioral_changes: `mainline init` without --actor-name now reads MAINLINE_ACTOR_NAME from the environment.
Success output prints a one-line note when the env-var fallback fires.
The 'No --actor-name passed' warning now mentions the env-var as an alternative path.
